sections.heading: What are Records? (Java 16+)
Records — validation and custom constructors
sections.content:
public record CreateDeliveryRequest(
    @NotBlank String driverId,
    @NotNull @Future LocalDateTime scheduledAt,
    @Size(min = 1, max = 50) List<StopRequest> stops
) {
    // Compact constructor — validation
    public CreateDeliveryRequest {
        if (stops == null) stops = List.of();
        stops = List.copyOf(stops);  // defensive copy (truly immutable)
    }

    // Custom factory method
    public static CreateDeliveryRequest urgent(String driverId, List<StopRequest> stops) {
        return new CreateDeliveryRequest(driverId, LocalDateTime.now().plusHours(1), stops);
    }
}

// Usage:
var req = new CreateDeliveryRequest("D-42", tomorrow, stops);
String driver = req.driverId();  // no "get" prefix!
When to use Records: • DTOs (request/response) • Value objects (Money, Coordinate, DateRange) • Kafka event payloads • Multi-value return types • Map keys (auto equals/hashCode) When NOT to use: • JPA entities (need mutable state + no-arg constructor) • Objects with complex lifecycle/behavior • When you need inheritance (records are final)

sections.heading: Sealed classes (Java 17)
Pattern matching with sealed types
sections.content:
// Java 21: exhaustive switch with pattern matching
public String describe(DeliveryEvent event) {
    return switch (event) {
        case DeliveryPlanned p -> "Planned for driver " + p.driverId();
        case DeliveryDispatched d -> "Dispatched on route " + d.route();
        case DeliveryCompleted c -> c.stopsDelivered() + " stops delivered";
        case DeliveryFailed f -> "Failed: " + f.reason();
        // No default needed! Compiler knows all cases are covered.
    };
}

// If you add a new event type → compiler ERROR in every switch
// Forces you to handle the new case everywhere (safe evolution)
REWE transport example: Delivery states are a CLOSED set. Using sealed + records ensures that every handler explicitly covers every possible state. Adding a new state becomes a compiler-assisted refactoring (not a runtime surprise).

sections.heading: Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16+)
sections.content:
// Before — cast after check (redundant):
if (event instanceof DeliveryCompleted) {
    DeliveryCompleted completed = (DeliveryCompleted) event;
    log.info("Delivered {} stops", completed.stopsDelivered());
}

// After — bind variable in the check:
if (event instanceof DeliveryCompleted completed) {
    log.info("Delivered {} stops", completed.stopsDelivered());
}

// Combined with switch (Java 21):
String message = switch (notification) {
    case SmsNotification sms -> "SMS to " + sms.phoneNumber();
    case EmailNotification email -> "Email to " + email.address();
    case PushNotification push -> "Push to device " + push.deviceId();
    default -> "Unknown channel";
};
Guards in patterns (Java 21): 
switch (delivery) {
    case Delivery d when d.status() == DELAYED && d.priority() == HIGH
        -> escalateToManager(d);
    case Delivery d when d.status() == DELAYED
        -> notifyPlanner(d);
    case Delivery d
        -> processNormally(d);
}

sections.heading: Text blocks (Java 15+)
sections.content:
// Before — string concatenation hell:
String query = "SELECT d.id, d.status, dr.name " +
    "FROM delivery d " +
    "JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id " +
    "WHERE d.status = :status " +
    "ORDER BY d.scheduled_at";

// After — text blocks (preserves formatting):
String query = """
    SELECT d.id, d.status, dr.name
    FROM delivery d
    JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id
    WHERE d.status = :status
    ORDER BY d.scheduled_at
    """;

// JSON templates:
String json = """
    {
        "deliveryId": "%s",
        "status": "%s",
        "timestamp": "%s"
    }
    """.formatted(id, status, Instant.now());
Use for: SQL queries, JSON templates, HTML fragments, multi-line log messages, test data

sections.heading: Switch expressions (Java 14+)
sections.content:
// Old switch (statement — can forget break):
String label;
switch (status) {
    case PLANNED: label = "Waiting"; break;
    case DISPATCHED: label = "On the way"; break;
    default: label = "Unknown"; break;
}

// New switch (expression — returns value, no break, no fall-through):
String label = switch (status) {
    case PLANNED -> "Waiting";
    case DISPATCHED -> "On the way";
    case IN_TRANSIT -> "Moving";
    case DELIVERED -> "Done";
    default -> "Unknown";
};

// Multi-line blocks:
int priority = switch (delivery.getType()) {
    case EXPRESS -> {
        log.info("Express delivery detected");
        yield 1;  // 'yield' returns value from block
    }
    case SAME_DAY -> { yield 2; }
    default -> { yield 3; }
};

sections.heading: Stream API — functional pipelines
sections.content:
Streams let you express data transformations declaratively: filter, map, flatMap, reduce, collect. They are lazy — intermediate operations build a pipeline; terminal operations trigger execution. 
// Find active deliveries with overdue ETA
List overdue = deliveries.stream()
    .filter(d -> d.getStatus() == ACTIVE)
    .filter(d -> d.getEta().isBefore(Instant.now()))
    .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Delivery::getEta))
    .toList(); // Java 16+ — immutable list

sections.heading: Collectors and grouping
Optional — explicit absence
Performance traps
sections.content: Streams are not always faster than loops. Avoid: parallel streams on small collections, streams that trigger N+1 database calls, unnecessary boxing with mapToInt/mapToLong, and collecting into a list only to iterate again. Use streams for readability when the pipeline is clear; use loops when mutation or early exit is simpler.

sections.heading: The problem with platform threads
sections.content: Each platform thread maps 1:1 to an OS thread (~1MB stack). A service handling 10,000 concurrent requests needs 10,000 OS threads — memory-prohibitive. The workaround was reactive/async code (CompletableFuture, WebFlux) which sacrifices readability.

sections.heading: Virtual threads — the solution
What changes for Spring Boot
sections.content:
Spring Boot 3.2+ supports virtual threads with one property: 
spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true
Every request handler runs on a virtual thread. Write simple blocking code — the JVM handles the scheduling. No need for WebFlux or reactive unless you need streaming/backpressure.

sections.heading: Pitfalls and when NOT to use
sections.content: Virtual threads do not help CPU-bound work — they shine for I/O-bound blocking. Avoid synchronized blocks (pins the carrier thread); use ReentrantLock instead. Thread-local variables work but can accumulate memory across millions of virtual threads — prefer scoped values (Java 21 preview). Connection pools (HikariCP) still limit concurrency to pool size — virtual threads queue at the pool, not at the OS.

sections.heading: REWE perspective
sections.content: A delivery tracking service handling thousands of concurrent GPS updates benefits immediately: simple blocking code, massive concurrency, no reactive complexity. The NEO platform can adopt virtual threads incrementally — enable the flag, monitor, and adjust pool sizes.

title: Java 17 → 21 Evolution — What Matters for REWE

sections.heading: Java 17 LTS highlights
sections.content: Sealed classes (restrict inheritance), pattern matching for instanceof, text blocks, helpful NullPointerExceptions (show which variable was null), strong encapsulation of JDK internals (no more --illegal-access). Java 17 is the baseline for Spring Boot 3.x and Jakarta EE 10.

sections.heading: Java 21 LTS highlights
sections.content: Virtual threads (GA), sequenced collections (SequencedMap, SequencedSet), record patterns in switch, string templates (preview), scoped values (preview). Java 21 is production-ready and the target for new greenfield services.

sections.heading: Migration strategy
sections.content: For existing services: stay on Java 17 LTS until dependencies support 21 (Spring Boot 3.2+, Hibernate 6.4+). For new services: start on Java 21. Key migration tasks: replace javax.* with jakarta.*, remove deprecated APIs (SecurityManager, Applet), fix reflection access to JDK internals, update build plugins.

sections.heading: Interview answer
sections.content: I track Java evolution pragmatically. Records and sealed classes improve domain modeling today. Virtual threads eliminate the reactive vs imperative debate for I/O-bound services. I adopt features that reduce complexity without creating migration risk for the team.

title: Records in Production — Immutability, Equality and Boundaries

sections.heading: A record is shallowly immutable
Choosing the right boundary
sections.content:
Records work well for request/response DTOs, events and small value objects with transparent structural equality. They are not a universal entity replacement: persistence identity, lazy proxies, lifecycle mutation and hidden invariants often make ordinary classes a better fit.

Ask whether the public meaning of the type is exactly its declared components. If yes, a record makes that contract explicit. If no, use a class whose API communicates behavior and lifecycle.

title: Pattern Matching — Exhaustiveness and Safe Branching

sections.heading: Scope, dominance and null
sections.content: A pattern variable exists only where Java can prove the match succeeded. Pattern-switch case order matters: a broad case Object value dominates later non-null cases and is rejected. Handle null deliberately with case null when it is valid input; it is not automatically handled by default. Use pattern matching to make branching over data shapes clearer, not to replace cohesive behavior with one growing central switch.

title: Streams in Production — Lifecycle and Performance

sections.heading: Pipelines are single-use and lazy
sections.content: Intermediate operations build a pipeline; a terminal operation triggers it. A stream is not a collection and cannot be reused after a terminal operation. Keep side effects out of peek, and close streams backed by I/O or database resources with try-with-resources. Parallel streams use the common ForkJoinPool by default. They are rarely a sound way to add blocking service concurrency: capacity, request context and downstream limits become harder to control. Prefer a loop when early exit or mutation is clearer.

title: Virtual Threads — Capacity Is Still a Design Problem

sections.heading: Protect scarce dependencies
sections.content:
Virtual threads make blocking I/O scale, but they do not make a database, remote API or connection pool unlimited. Create a virtual thread per task, then separately bound scarce resources with a connection pool, semaphore, rate limit and end-to-end timeout.

Observe latency percentiles, downstream saturation, carrier utilization and pinning with the JDK you actually deploy. Modern JDKs removed the old general monitor-pinning problem, but native and foreign calls can still pin. Measure rather than applying outdated blanket rules.

title: Upgrading Java Safely — Runtime, Tooling and Rollout

sections.heading: Treat an upgrade as an operational change
sections.content: Inventory build plugins, annotation processors, agents, native libraries, container images and dependencies that use JDK internals. Test the deployable image on the target JDK, not only local compilation. Baseline startup, memory, GC, throughput and latency; then use a canary with rollback criteria. Separate runtime adoption from language-feature refactoring so a regression has a clear cause. Preview features require --enable-preview at compile and runtime and should be an explicit team policy.
